text: The laparoscopic view reveals two large hernias in the suprapubic region, with part of the bladder protruding into the hernia sac.
The surgeon dissects the peritoneum to separate the bladder from the hernia sac, preparing for mesh placement with half positioned intraperitoneally and half preperitoneally.

The surgeon carefully pushes the bladder down to create a preperitoneal pocket. A large hernia is present, and a 15 by 30 centimeter mesh is planned for placement. A small portion of the mesh will be positioned retro pubic and fixed to the Cooper ligament, while the remaining mesh will be placed intraperitoneally. The peritoneum, adherent due to scar tissue, is slowly separated from the abdominal wall using a harmonic device.

A semi-traumatic grasper is used to pull the peritoneum, gradually creating the pre-peritoneal space. A dense polypropylene and polyurethane mesh is prepared to repair the defect associated with rectus diastasis.

care should be taken that we should keep ourselves as near as possible to the abdominal wall because in these patients the anterior wall of the bladder is quite thin and sometime it may be possible that it will rupture. Methylene blue is also injected sometime at the end of the surgery into the bladder to see the integrity of the bladder.

The surgeon is working on the left side, carefully separating the peritoneum downwards in the midline, where significant scar tissue is present. The previous laparotomy scar reveals visible Prolene sutures. The right Cooper ligament is exposed, and the bladder has been fully displaced downwards. Minimal energy devices are used during this process.

The surgeon pulls out the entire hernia sac, revealing the Cooper ligament on the left side in the midline. The hernia is embedded in the surrounding tissues, requiring separation of the adherent peritoneum due to previous scar tissue.
The surgeon carefully separates the peritoneum from the abdominal wall, effectively pulling down the bladder and hernia sac, revealing the underlying anatomical structures.

The surgeon reaches the pubic symphysis bone, with the bladder and urethra visible in the middle. The peritoneum is reflected on either side. A mesh is introduced into the pocket, with half placed pre-peritoneal and half intraperitoneal. The mesh is pushed into position and fixed onto the Cooper ligament on both sides, using multiple tackers to secure it all around.

The surgeon positions the mesh with the blue polyurethane side facing the bowel and the white polypropylene side facing the abdominal wall. Using a titanium ProTack device, the mesh is fixed in place, ensuring tackers do not fire over the inferior epigastric vessels. The abdominal wall is supported during this process.
The surgeon positions the mesh over the bladder, ensuring it covers the area adequately. The mesh is fixed in place, and the bladder is carefully inspected for any signs of injury.

In the middle where the rectus muscle was located, a couple of tackers are fired to secure the mesh. The mesh can be easily separated due to the polyurethane material, which reduces the risk of adhesion.
In this case you cannot expect to cover the entire mesh with the plutonium and it is not necessary also because this mesh has very little chance of adhesion. Thank you very much.
